del:hasContribution.del:text:
The vote will be held today by secret ballot.
Concerning the joint decision of INTA and ITRE committees to enter into interinstitutional negotiations pursuant to Rule脗聽72脗聽(1), announced that the opening of the session on Monday 20 October, I have received no request for a vote in Parliament by Members of political groups reaching the medium threshold.
The INTA and ITRE committees may therefore start negotiations.

I would like to remind you that this will be in the format that we've been putting into practice, where there will be no speakers' list.
So after the first round of speakers on behalf of the groups, Members who have received speaking time from their political groups or the Non-Attached Secretariat will be called to speak and will be reminded of their allotted speaking time.
Please keep an eye on the screens, and the normal rules regarding blue cards and the catch-the-eye procedure will apply during the debate.

A full agenda is expected for the European Council, with issues from foreign policy and defence and security to competitiveness and the green transition. Leaders will also talk about housing and migration issues that are particularly important for European citizens.
The European Council will discuss the situation in Ukraine as Russia continues its relentless attacks against Ukraine, civilians and civil infrastructure. Leaders will focus on stepping up support to Ukraine, including financial support for the coming years. They will also discuss efforts to further increase pressure on Russia and reaffirm the EU support for Ukraine's accession path.脗聽President Zelensky is expected to intervene at the beginning of the discussions about Ukraine.
Leaders will assess the latest developments in the Middle East. Peace seems to be within reach in Gaza after two years of war and human suffering. Leaders will discuss the latest developments, including the US 20-point peace plan, the catastrophic humanitarian situation and famine in Gaza, and the worsening situation in the West Bank.
On European security and defence, leaders will build on the fruitful discussions at the informal meeting in Copenhagen earlier this month. The European Council is expected to take decisions on speeding up work to ramp up Europe's defence readiness, including commitments and concrete decisions on capability projects and governance. The Roadmap on Defence Readiness 2030, which was presented on Thursday by the Commission and the High Representative, will inform leaders discussions.
Hybrid attacks and drone sightings near our critical infrastructures and hostile incursions into our airspace remind us that we must speed up our work to achieve European common readiness by 2030.
Turning now to competitiveness and the transition, leaders will focus on three main topics. First, stepping up our efforts to deliver on simplification. There has been progress so far, but we need to move up a gear in cutting bureaucratic burdens and preventing new ones. This agenda is a high political priority. The Council has mandates ready for the core remainder of Omnibus 1 on sustainability reporting and due diligence, and for Omnibus 4. And the Council stands ready to start the negotiations as soon as the European Parliament adopts its position. We should conclude work quickly. The expectation is that the European Parliament shares the sense of urgency for the simplification agenda.
Second, leaders will have a strategic debate on how to reach our climate objectives and at the same time strengthen Europe's competitiveness. The focus will be on supporting our industries and citizens on the path to carbon neutrality in 2050. As you know, enabling a successful green transition is one of the Danish Presidency's core priorities.
Third, the digital transition, which is key to creating resilient and a competitive solutions for citizens and businesses, and crucial in today's global context. To ensure Europe's digital competitiveness and sovereignty, we need regulatory and technological autonomy, reflecting our interests and values while using the full potential of new technologies. The topic of housing will be on the European Council agenda for the first time. Affordable housing has become a growing social and economic concern across the Union. Also for middle income citizens and younger generations. Leaders will assess what can be done at European level to support efforts at national, regional and local levels to improve access to affordable and decent housing. That discussion will also provide guidance for the Commission's upcoming European Affordable Housing Plan. Migration is another important topic for both European citizens and leaders. The European Council will get back to this topic to ensure that we work fast on all priority files.
Finally, the European Council will reflect on the recent elections in Moldova, which were held against the backdrop of Russia's many hybrid attacks to weaken the country's democratic institutions. The European Council will also reaffirm the EU's support for Moldova's accession path, as well as for the country's resilience and stability. All in all, we expect a substantial European Council meeting where leaders will address many crucial and interlinked challenges facing Europe today.
Ensuring our security and competitiveness goes hand in hand with increasing our efforts to harness the digital and green transformation. This is also a key priority for the Danish Presidency, and we continue to work with the European Parliament in resolving these important issues.

But today I would like to focus on another topic that will be at the centre of tomorrow's European Council, and that is the issue of competitiveness and decarbonisation.
Let me start by observing simple facts. Globally, the clean transition is in full swing. If you look at some figures from last year, more than 580 gigawatts in renewable energy capacity were added globally 芒聙聯 a new record for the third year in a row. Clean tech markets around the world are booming. The global market for batteries is expected to double in size over the next five years. The global market for wind turbines continues to rise by over 10脗聽% every year. And in developing economies across Asia, Africa and Latin America, last year the sales of electric cars have risen by 60脗聽%.
This is not just good news for the climate 芒聙聯 the rise of clean tech in Europe is also good news for our energy security, and it is a great economic opportunity. There is a reason why China is so massively investing in electric vehicles, batteries and clean tech. Of course, they see the economic opportunity and they see what the future markets will be. There is a reason why in the Middle East, which is actually the global home of fossil fuels, renewables increased fivefold in the last five years. The Middle East is dominant today on the energy market, and they tell me they want to be dominant on the energy markets in the future. And there is a reason why the Global South is choosing clean energy, because they want to get rid of choking dependencies and bring in energy that is not just clean but home-grown and, for them, affordable.
So the question for us is whether we want to reap the benefits of this global clean tech boom, or just let others profit from it. I have not the slightest doubt about the answer. Europe is a global leader in clean innovation. Let me give you some figures. For instance, 40脗聽% of all wind technology companies worldwide are here in the European Union. We have a very skilled workforce and a long tradition of industrial primacy. Last year, Europe's clean tech exports reached EUR脗聽80脗聽billion. This means they have almost tripled in just six years.
But others are not sleeping. China exports almost twice as much clean tech as we do, and this risks pushing our industry out of the strategic markets 芒聙聯 including our own. We cannot afford to fall into new and dangerous dependencies. And in recent years, we have all witnessed what happens when a single country gains control over the supply of a critical product or technology. At any moment, those dependencies can be turned into instruments of pressure. Export restrictions can be imposed overnight. Supply routes can be disrupted. And factories here in Europe can be forced to slow down or even shut down.
So I think we should be very clear and very vigilant. Europe must control the technologies that will shape our future, the manufacturing that underpins our prosperity and the critical raw materials. We should also be very clear in the determination that no country should hold the ability to undermine our economic security.
It is important for me to address this here in the hemicycle, because in the coming days, I will also engage very closely with European leaders and international partners, as a crisis in the supply of critical raw materials is no longer a distant risk. It is on our doorstep. We have already taken important steps. But now we must accelerate, decisively and urgently. We need a faster, more reliable supply of critical raw materials, both here in Europe and with trusted partners. I will be ready to propose further measures to ensure Europe's economic security, and I will accelerate what we have already put in motion.
So, to be very clear among us: Europe must be in control of strategic clean technologies and manufacturing, because clean tech is not just a tool for cutting emissions, it is key to our competitiveness and key to the independence of the European Union, and we really have to fight for it.
This is why we must refocus on supporting clean products that are made here in Europe. As the International Energy Agency reminds us, there is a cautionary tale that we must not forget. It is the story of our solar industry at the beginning of this century. Europe was a global leader in solar, but heavily subsidised Chinese competitors started to outprice Europe's young industry, and today China controls 90脗聽% of the global market.
This time we should learn our lesson. And let me quickly address three ways of doing so. First, we will introduce a 'made in Europe' criteria for public procurement in certain strategic sectors 芒聙聯 just keep in mind public procurement in Europe amounts to 14脗聽% of the European GDP. This is massive financial firepower controlled by European governments, and we should make better use of it in order to create stable demand for clean, 'made in Europe' products.
My second point: we should ensure that new foreign investments in our industry are truly in Europe's interest. These investments are much needed, without any question, but they should create good jobs here for European workers and they should add value to Europeans right here in the European Union 芒聙聯 not just for others.
And my third point is we will step up support for some strategic sectors. For instance, with a battery booster to support production in Europe and with a new set of initiatives for the automotive sector. We will send a very clear political signal to these industries. And that is: you are critical to Europe's future, and your future will be made in Europe. That is critical for us.
Honourable Members, a final point on competitiveness. We all know that high energy prices continue to put a strain on our competitiveness. And we all know the main reason for that: it is our dependency on imported fossil fuels and, therefore, our dependency on a volatile global market. Just look at the data across Europe; the lowest prices for energy are in countries that produce abundant low carbon energy 芒聙聯 be it in solar, wind, hydropower, geothermal or nuclear.脗聽So the way ahead is clear: we must continue to invest in home-grown clean energy.
But besides our dependency on fossil fuels, there are also other reasons that prevent energy prices from going down, and we have to look at these causes too. We have, for example, to improve our energy infrastructure. Therefore, we will come with the grids package and the new energy highways initiative, and you know that we reviewed the rules for cohesion. Cohesion funding can also be invested for grids and interconnectors to improve the energy situation in the European Union. All this requires action at European level 芒聙聯 and we are on it.
But another action is required at Member State level. Yesterday, the Commission presented a set of new, very concrete actions to cut energy bills in Europe. Let me just give you one example, and that is taxes. While energy costs are going down, taxes on energy are going up again. Look at the taxes our industry is paying on electricity; these taxes are 15 times higher than taxes on gas. And that cannot be. Or I give you two other figures: if you look at the energy bills on average for our industry in the European Union, 34脗聽% of those bills are taxes; and if you look at the energy bills of our households, on average in the European Union, 42脗聽% of those bills are taxes. So let's work together to bring these taxes down and allow our businesses and households to switch to using domestically produced electricity rather than imported fuels.
To be clear, I know, of course, that Europe will continue to use oil and gas for years to come as a basis, without any question, but that will never be a source of advantage for Europe's industry, for its consumers or for the planet. And with that in mind, we will also propose to cooperate to speed up permitting, to make full use of our State Aid rules and, of course, as I said, of our cohesion funds. Our industries and households cannot wait longer. They need affordable energy in this difficult geoeconomic climate, and they need it now.
Honourable Members, competitiveness has been a central focus of this Commission from day one. But our competitiveness agenda needs every one of us 芒聙聯 governments and industry, Member States and European institutions. We all need the political will to move forward, because inaction is simply not an option. We all need faster decisions, because the clean industrial race will only get faster. And the choice is very clear: we can either stand still and fall behind, or we can build the jobs of the future right here in Europe. So let's make it happen together.
Thank you very much, and long live Europe!

Meanwhile, Trump and Xi are stepping out of global actions. Fight against climate change is now on our shoulders, but we are quite isolated on global level. And Putin is sending his drones towards Europe, another reality. Poland is already shielding our borders in the east. Ukrainian warns us more are coming and Europe must be prepared.
That is, in a way, a short picture about where we are: Ukraine, climate change, the future of our economy. Though the next European Council must lead, there is no excuse anymore, it has to deliver.
It is a moment of truth in front of us in this autumn, and it is a moment of truth on Europe's global roll. So Donald Trump managed to have now a peace agreement in Gaza, and we hope that it will stay. But let's be honest. Europe was in the whole process of the Gaza talks an observer. We didn't play a real role there. And now the question for the European Council is on the table, do we now play a role when we continue to talk about Ukraine?
To be concrete, from the European Council, we expect that we are clear in financing the weapons, that the Russian assets must be used for this, and also the state, Ukraine, needs further support from our side to survive, and we have to find ways on the Council side to speak with one voice. We know on European, on global level, we only have one voice or no voice.
And just one aspect on the latest development on this, when we see the postponement of possible talks between Trump and Putin, the Budapest consideration, you know, in my country, the AfD is heavily attacking us, that we need more diplomatic efforts to approach Putin and talk with him and find a way to talk with him.
Again, we can underline that more red carpet than in Alaska is not possible. Even Trump tried it now to please and to invite Putin to come to the negotiation table. So even the right extreme in Europe must understand that Putin has only one interest, and that is destroying our European way of life.
The moment of truth is also in front of us when it is about defence. We spend in the next ten years EUR 6.4 trillion as Europeans. That is the same amount of money as the Americans will invest. And that is why I think the expectation from our citizens is that, after ten years, we have the same military capacity as Europeans, as the Americans have. That means we have to explore the European added value, the drones initiative, the missile defence, the sky shield, all the initiatives now presented by the Commission.
I also want to thank Andrius Kubilius for his efforts are really great steps forwards. I want to underline that for me, it's also the moment now to come back to the idea of Adenauer, De Gasperi and Schuman to talk really about the European Army, to do the necessary steps together.
And it is also a moment of truth for our competitiveness. Omnibus 1 will be voted this week in this House. We do it together, but we have to do much, much more. We have to do things that our businesses feel is well, that we are attractive for the best minds on global level, to come to Europe, we need urgently growth on European level. That is why single market and also the idea of an ambitious trade policy is at the centre of the EPP. And that's why I thank also the Commission for the Commission Work Programme 26, where you are covering these issues.
Finally, the link to decarbonisation 芒聙聭 the 2040 target. Dear colleagues, it's Macron, it's Donald Tusk who are going this week to the European Council table and are asking the questions are we ready for the 2040 target, 90脗聽%? Yes or no? I saw yesterday a French/Spanish initiative to take the combustion engine question again on the table. So as the EPP leader I can only say welcome in the club, we always asked for this. We have to be ambitious but pragmatic in the implementation of the climate change, and that is what we want to see also from the leaders there, the workers in Europe, and also those who are socially not as strong as we are, need a proper answer, so that the transition in front of us will be not on the cost of social and of job losses in the European Union.
That is the moment of truth in front of us. And that is why we feel that it is not business as usual. We as European People's Party, we are ready to deliver. We do so and I hope that the Council will do the same this week.

Y ma脙卤ana, por primera vez, el Consejo Europeo se prepara para pedir a la Comisi脙鲁n que presente un plan europeo de vivienda asequible. Es un paso hist脙鲁rico, y no habr脙颅a sido posible sin el trabajo incansable del Grupo Socialdem脙鲁crata, porque ha sido la socialdemocracia la que ha puesto la vivienda en el centro de la agenda europea, con un principio claro: tener un hogar es un derecho, no un privilegio.
Entre 2010 y 2025, los precios de la vivienda en Europa subieron un 60脗聽%. M脙隆s de la mitad de los residentes en zonas urbanas dicen que el acceso a la vivienda es su principal preocupaci脙鲁n y m脙隆s de un mill脙鲁n de europeos siguen sin techo; por eso proponemos una respuesta ambiciosa: 300脗聽000 millones de euros en inversi脙鲁n europea para viviendas dignas, una reforma de las ayudas de Estado para facilitar el acceso a las familias de renta media y baja, la regulaci脙鲁n de los alquileres de corta duraci脙鲁n, el refuerzo de los derechos de los inquilinos y un pr脙漏stamo europeo para la renovaci脙鲁n sostenible con criterios sociales. Porque una casa no es solo un tejado, es donde creamos una familia, donde cuidamos a nuestros seres queridos y construimos esperanza.
Y esa esperanza debe guiar tambi脙漏n nuestra acci脙鲁n frente a la emergencia clim脙隆tica. La Uni脙鲁n Europea solo ser脙隆 fuerte si lidera la transici脙鲁n verde con justicia social. Por eso defendemos mantener el objetivo del 90脗聽% de reducci脙鲁n de las emisiones para 2040, transformarlo en ley y garantizar que nadie se quede atr脙隆s.
Queremos una ley europea de adaptaci脙鲁n que nos prepare ante las olas de calor, las sequ脙颅as o los incendios; queremos una Directiva de transici脙鲁n justa que proteja a los trabajadores con formaci脙鲁n, con di脙隆logo y con anticipaci脙鲁n, y un fondo social para el clima m脙隆s ambicioso, que conecte la justicia clim脙隆tica con la justicia social.
Y mientras construimos un futuro sostenible, no podemos ignorar el presente dolor y destrucci脙鲁n en Gaza. El alto el fuego y la liberaci脙鲁n de los rehenes son imprescindibles, pero la paz solo ser脙隆 posible si se conduce a un Estado palestino viable. Y la paz tampoco puede construirse sobre la impunidad: el Consejo debe actuar suspendiendo el Acuerdo de Asociaci脙鲁n con Israel, porque Netanyahu debe rendir cuentas por su genocidio, y la justicia no es negociable.
Y frente al criminal de Putin debemos ser igualmente firmes: sabemos que no quiere la paz y solo la derrota lo sentar脙隆 a negociar; por eso debemos aprobar ya el 19.脗潞 paquete de sanciones, confiscar los 200脗聽000 millones en activos rusos congelados y prohibir, definitivamente, las importaciones de petr脙鲁leo y gas, tal y como este Parlamento ha propuesto. El Consejo no puede seguir retrasando lo inevitable.
Se脙卤or脙颅as, construyamos una Europa que vuelva a mirar a los ojos a sus ciudadanos y ciudadanas, una Europa que ofrezca techo a quien no lo tiene, paz a quien sufre la guerra, trabajo a quien busca dignidad y futuro a quienes solo conocen la incertidumbre.
Porque cuando Europa se atreve a ser valiente, vuelve a ser cre脙颅ble; porque cuando Europa demuestra ser humana, vuelve a ser grande.

脙聙 tous les chefs d'entreprise, artisans, commer脙搂ants, 脙聽 tous les travailleurs, je veux aujourd'hui parler d'un autre type de fiscalit脙漏, d'une taxe invisible qui ne dit pas son nom, celle des normes que l'on vous impose par milliers. Cet imp脙麓t papier, c'est l'arme d'une bureaucratie sans visage qui complexifie la vie de ceux qui produisent, qui les immobilise derri脙篓re un bureau des heures durant. Ce fardeau normatif, fabriqu脙漏 脙聽 Bruxelles, puis surtranspos脙漏 dans nos capitales, co脙禄te pr脙篓s de 7脗聽% de la richesse cr脙漏脙漏e en Europe, soit 1脗聽200脗聽milliards d'euros chaque ann脙漏e.
Ces textes 脙聽 rallonge tuent la comp脙漏titivit脙漏 de nos entreprises et de nos producteurs, minent le pouvoir d'achat des familles et d脙漏sincite chacun 脙聽 investir et 脙聽 innover. Disons-le, l'Europe est devenue un fardeau normatif: CSRD, CS3D, RED, REACH, GSR, IED, DSA et j'en passe. Toutes nos usines s'en vont. Mais l'industrie 脙聽 normes, elle, ne conna脙庐t pas la crise.
Dans l'Europe d'Ursula脗聽von脗聽der脗聽Leyen, dans l'Europe de Macron, la croissance est devenue taboue et la d脙漏croissance, elle, est sur toutes les l脙篓vres. Nos entrepreneurs, nos agriculteurs, nos artisans, nos industriels sont 脙聽 tort consid脙漏r脙漏s comme des suspects par une technocratie qui entend remettre notre avenir dans la d脙漏pendance aux puissances 脙漏trang脙篓res. 脙聙 force de poser des verrous sur la production nationale, vous prenez le risque de nous faire sortir de l'histoire. Pendant que les 脙聣tats-Unis, la Chine ou l'Inde avancent, l'Europe, elle, regarde le train passer.
Il est temps de r脙漏armer notre continent et nos nations dans la guerre 脙漏conomique mondiale. Il est temps de lib脙漏rer nos entreprises, de lib脙漏rer nos forces productives, de suspendre sans d脙漏lai le pacte vert, de simplifier l'脙漏conomie nationale, de redonner fi脙篓rement 脙聽 nos entrepreneurs les conditions du succ脙篓s, de la croissance et de la r脙漏ussite. Vivre libre ou dispara脙庐tre, tel est le d脙漏fi existentiel qui se pose 脙聽 l'Europe. Les Patriotes ont d脙漏j脙聽 fait leur choix.

Pani przewodnicz脛聟ca m脙鲁wi脜聜a, 脜录e konkurencyjno脜聸脛聡 jest najwa脜录niejsza. Yet often the problems start at home. We see that the fact that we have 40脗聽% tariffs on the goods and more than 120脗聽% tariffs on the services within the European Union. We are suffocating our companies. We talk about the need for clean tech, which is really, really big, but we are actually putting obstacles in front of the companies who want to grow in Europe.
So please, dear Council, put one important thing on your agenda that's missing: how to stop putting obstacles between the Member States. Everybody talks about cutting back bureaucracy on the European level. What about stopping unnecessary bureaucracy you put it on top of the European rules because people face hardship. Despite all the blaming of Brussels, people trust Europe more than ever and we need to deliver. And it's time for the Council to actually start delivering. We need stronger Europe. We need a super Europe that actually cares about the people, cares about the competitiveness, that housing is a right, not a commodity, that really gets independent from the imported fossil energy. All that 芒聙聭脗聽and it was great to hear it from the President 芒聙聭 is the right way forward.
And I think the majority of this House is ready to support it. But what we see often is Council putting obstacles. So please, for example, on the ETS, make sure all the money goes back to people and businesses and just doesn't end up in the state coffers. And I could go on. First, Ukraine: our support must continue politically, financially and also through reconstruction. Europe must stay united here to help Ukraine, and we have to do everything to secure a just and lasting peace. And that can only be achieved, as I see it, on Ukraine's terms.
Second, security and resilience: Europe must better protect its citizens by safeguarding critical infrastructure and countering hybrid threats.
Third, the Middle East: The European Union must play a role here. We have to play a role in diplomatic, political and economics. We have to work here and to continue to work on a two-state solution.
Finally, migration: Austria supports a balanced implementation of the new pact on migration and asylum, with strong borders, effective returns and deepening partnerships with countries of origin and transit.
So this European Council must send a clear message: Europe delivers with unity.

So let me just react on a few most recurring issues and points you've been raising in your speeches.
First, I would like to reassure you how important the competitiveness for the European Commission is. And I think the last intervention on this was Madam Grapini, and we are working together very well on the Customs Union reform, and therefore I am using this opportunity to underscore that also, the President, not only yesterday when we have been presenting the Commission work programme, but also this morning elaborated in great detail on a clean tech, on the importance of the industry for Europe, but also on the crucial role we all have to play in cutting red tape for our economic operators.
And therefore, I just would like to highlight one additional aspect which we, I think covered more yesterday than today. And this is our focus on farmers, fishers and agrifood sector.
My colleague, Commissioner for Agriculture Christophe Hansen, together with the whole college, is working very hard on implementing the new strategic vision for agriculture. We are looking from every aspect how to further minimise reporting obligations and how to motivate young people to become farmers. It was the proposal the Commission put on the table just yesterday. You would agree with me that the European Union is an agri-food superpower, and we will do everything possible to keep it that way also in the future.
I also would like to thank honourable Members for the focus of our debate today on the need to address jointly energy prices, upgrade of our energy grids and to tackle the bureaucracy on all levels. Mr Nica was referring to energy prices and my compatriot, Mr Hojs脙颅k, Vice-President of the Parliament, was focusing on the importance of the joint combined effort. We have to do our utmost if it comes to simplification from the EU level. But of course the Member States have to help us even more than today to stop the bureaucratic gold plating on the national and regional level.
Mario Draghi was right. If we can get rid of national barriers, if we can remove the obstacles on our single market, it would more than compensate for the recent tariff barriers being erected on the global trade level.
Despite of all this, I would like to reassure the honourable Members and this House that we are working very hard to bring to our economic operators, farmers, industry, SMEs, new market access new business opportunities through our ambitious agenda of free trade agreements. We want to make sure that we would help to secure our independence by introducing, in all our agreements, very ambitious chapters on the critical raw materials, and we are extending a hand to all our partners, be it under global gateway projects or other agreements which we are proposing to them to work on mutually advantageous projects that would create jointly value added in those countries and provide the necessary critical raw material supplies also for the European economy.
Mr Van Lanschot, under the catch-the-eye procedure, was rightly referring to the fact that housing is now new focus for all the interinstitutional cooperation, and we in the European Commission are very happy about that. That it's a priority for European Parliament, it's a priority for the Council. The leaders under the European Council setting will be discussing exactly the issue, which is so problematic for young people across Europe. And I believe that this political concentration and attention given to this topic will bring us also concrete results.
And I would like also to react on one of the latest interventions by Ms Temido脗聽and Mr Oliveira on poverty. I think just yesterday we had a full debate on the Commission work programme, and I am sure that you will see that if it comes to anti-poverty strategy, if you would look for enhanced child guarantee, it is all there. And we know how important it is for our youth, how important it is for people of Europe. And I believe that jointly with you and with the Council, we will deliver on this very important priority.
And to conclude, Madam President, I would just like to thank the honourable Members for the debate, and I am sure that the questions related to defence, security and foreign policy will be covered by my esteemed colleague, Minister Bjerre, as it is in accordance with our Treaties, and therefore, I just would like to thank everyone for the discussions, for the questions, and I believe, for support of the very ambitious agenda for the future of Europe.

Many of you referred to Ukraine. I want to say that is also a very, very important agenda for the Council. We need to further increase our pressure on Russia. We have to keep supporting Ukraine and we have to keep up the EU accession plan for Ukraine. Also, it is of utmost importance that we ramp up Europe's defence capacities and that we support the roadmap on defence readiness.
Many of you also touched upon the green transition and competitiveness, and I think the debate illustrates quite well that green transition and competitiveness goes hand in hand. We have been leading in many green technologies in Europe and green technologies are the future. But if we don't make the right balances and if we don't have the right means in reaching our climate goals, then we risk overwhelming our businesses with burdens. That is why both simplification and the green transition are important topics for the leaders tomorrow.
Also, several of you touched upon housing. It is clear from the debate that housing is an important topic. It is also for the Council. It has become a growing social and economic concern for many Member States. While respecting the national competences, there is still scope for cooperation and that is why it is now on the agenda. It's on the agenda for the first time and the Danish Presidency aims to reach Council conclusions in December on this matter.
Many other important topics were also brought up in this debate, for instance migration. You all know that is also a key topic for the Danish Presidency. I think all these topics mentioned today also show that it is a quite substantive agenda the leaders have tomorrow. But there are also many, many challenges that Europe is faced with and we need to solve them together.

Five years on from 9脗聽August脗聽2020, Lukashenka has chosen the path of accelerating repression over that of engagement and reform. The recent release of political prisoners, including prominent Belarusian activists, journalists, as well as EU nationals, is a positive step. We are especially relieved that our colleagues from the EU Delegation in Minsk were amongst those released. Since 2020 and before, supporting the victims of repression in Belarus has been at the core of our policy. Our victims facility has provided relocation, legal, medical and psychological support to victims of repression and political prisoners in more than 7脗聽500 cases. I can say today that we are already providing support to the majority of those released in September. If further releases take place, the EU will be there to support those who need it.
Despite the good news, we cannot ignore the harsh realities. Over 1脗聽200 individuals remain arbitrarily detained for political reasons. This is an increase compared to when we last spoke about Belarus here in July. Included in that number is Andrzej Poczobut, who you have honoured as a nominee for this year's Sakharov Prize. Thank you for highlighting this and other cases in your resolutions and declarations over the last year.脗聽The case of political prisoner Mikalai Statkevich is particularly concerning. After refusing to be removed from Belarus in the release on 11脗聽September, his whereabouts are unknown and a group of UN experts have warned he could be the victim of enforced disappearance. We call on Belarusian authorities to immediately make clear where he is being held and, ultimately, to release him without enforcing his deportation from the Belarus.
The EU condemns Belarus's continued support to Russia's illegal war of aggression against Ukraine. Recently, this has involved supplying oil and gas to supplement Russia's energy supply. Since 2021, Belarus has instrumental migration flows as a political tool to pressure the EU's external borders. The EU stands in solidarity with the affected Member States.
In July, in this House, I made clear what we expect to see from the Belarusian authorities. We call for the immediate and unconditional release of all political prisoners and end of repression. The Belarusian regime must put an end to arbitrary detentions, halt the creation and enforcement of repressive laws and stop politically motivated prosecutions, including trials held脗聽in absentia. We call on the regime to respect the rights of the detainees, in line with Belarus's international obligations. Belarusian authorities must also stop incommunicado detentions and ill treatment of prisoners.
Until we see a fundamental shift in these areas, we will continue to apply pressure on the regime while supporting the people of Belarus and their democratic aspirations. The EU has imposed several packages of restrictive measures, both sectoral and individual, targeting those responsible for repression and human rights abuses, misconduct of the election process, complicity in the war against Ukraine and inciting and organising illegal border crossings.
The EU is also leading accountability efforts at the regional and international level, and supporting accountability mechanisms like the International Accountability Platform for Belarus. At the same time, the EU continues to stand in solidarity with the people of Belarus. We have already mobilised EUR脗聽170 million to support the people. We will continue to support Belarusians, independent media, civil society and those committed to building a peaceful and democratic future.
We are ready to work with you, the European Parliament, our Member States and international partners to support the people of Belarus. We are in this for the long haul and we hope we can count on your support in this endeavour.

Both are journalists currently in prison on trumped-up charges simply for doing their work and for speaking out against injustice. Their courage has made them symbols of the struggle for freedom and democracy. This House stands with them and with all those who continue to demand freedom.

The stakes are high. Americans are ready to pay for the services of a middleman. The middleman gets what he wants, but he gives out hostages in return. At one point in these talks, a few months ago, Lukashenka promised to release one 'big name' prisoner 芒聙聯 that was my big lucky moment. At the time, my name was the most well-known in the West, due to my wife's efforts. Lukashenka was trying hard to impress President Trump. That's why I got out.
I managed to survive five years of solitary confinement in the hell of the Belarusian gulag. Some of those I met there were not as lucky. We have been losing the best of our people. As for me, I know I would never have survived 20 years of my full sentence under those same conditions. The fate of Aleksei Navalny was very much in store for me.
There are still around 1脗聽400 political prisoners in Belarus waiting for their turn to be free, and the number is growing by the day. We must get them all out. This issue has become my priority.
But what about the country of Belarus? Is it a lost cause?
During my time in prison, the dictator kept me completely isolated: no letters, no visits, no contact with the outside world. The only news I received was from state propaganda on the radio. It has been four months since my release, and I am quickly catching up with the world. But it is painful to see that things have gotten worse. The business of changing the regime in Belarus, that I started with other leaders five years ago, remains unfinished.
In the summer of 2020, the people of Belarus showed they've had enough. The number of people which took to the streets against Lukashenka was astounding. If you were to scale up this number relative to the population in Russia, it would be the equivalent of 15脗聽million people. No chance Putin would have survived such an open rebellion. For a short time, there was a moment we thought we could win. Some of Lukashenka's government officials were expressing sympathy, but stayed hesitant. They didn't flip to the side of the people. Why? Because when the time came, the Russians showed up for the fight 芒聙聯 Europeans did not.
Because of Europe's hesitation, Belarus 芒聙聯 a beautiful nation with strong Western, European values, this nation which was totally ready to become a member of the EU 芒聙聯 was left alone and Moscow took over. This was not only tragic and unfair, it was also a serious strategic mistake. By failing to stop Putin in Belarus, Europe made the war in Ukraine almost inevitable. Hundreds of thousands killed, billions of dollars wasted, and we don't see the end of it.
Even if President Trump does the impossible and the fighting stops tomorrow, a hybrid war would continue, and trillions of euros will be spent on European defence. Just think how stupid it is 芒聙聯 it could have cost a tiny fraction of this budget to change the game in Belarus in 2020. But that ship has sailed.
So what should be done now? How do we win this war? And what role does Belarus play in this?
In this complex, potentially very long hybrid war, victory will come only to those who take the initiative. My message is simple: Europe not only needs to become an active player, Europe needs to become an active player in Belarus. Lukashenka is a cunning survivor, but he is not immortal. The transfer of power in Belarus is imminent.
Here is the reality: since 2020, the people of Belarus have not accepted the dictator. They did not love him back. They did not revolt because they are simply being wise, careful and realistic. They understand that without any real allies, we are fighting a losing battle. Of course, Belarusians would love to join NATO, but after what happened in Ukraine, Belarusians will never believe we will ever be accepted. We are not that na脙炉ve.
So how do we solve this problem? What's the formula? The answer can be found in Europe's own history. Think about Finland 芒聙聯 think about Finland before it joined NATO. Finland learned how to live next to a difficult neighbour and still built a successful independent country without going to war. It was a careful balance, a smart strategy. Europe would benefit if Belarus became a neutral bridge between the EU and Russia. Belarusians would benefit as well. That would be a good thing for both sides, a definite win-win.
But, of course, Putin sees Belarus very differently. He sees it as a buffer zone, a military base he can use to pressure his neighbours, a country with limited independence, something like Chechnya, but in central Europe.
So how do we pull this off? There is only one solution: we need a third player in this political game. For many decades, the United States has saved you hundreds of billions of dollars each year, holding the security umbrella while Europeans were busy building a social welfare state. But today, America cannot be fighting on all three battlefields 芒聙聯 in the Middle East, in Europe and in Asia 芒聙聯 at the same time.
It is Europe, not the United States, that should take a leading role on its eastern borders. It is Europe that should become the main partner in helping Belarus move toward democracy. You are big, you are strong, your population is a whopping 500脗聽million, your GDP makes up 17脗聽% of the world total. Your total spending on helping Ukraine is a mere 0.1脗聽% 芒聙聯 something tells me you can be doing a lot more. You just need confidence, you just need leadership, you just need to get serious.
We, the Belarusian democratic movement, have a plan. A plan for how to become the next Finland of eastern Europe. You have the tools, resources and the strength to make it real. It's your call now, Europe. Don't let us down 芒聙聯 not like the last time.
Zhyve Belarus!

So we will vote on the decision to enter interinstitutional negotiations. It is by secret ballot and the vote is open.

I take note of the differences in the positions of our two institutions concerning the draft budget 2026 presented by the Commission. Consequently, in my capacity of President of the Council, I agree that the President of the European Parliament convenes the Conciliation Committee as required in Article 314(4)(c) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union.

'Recognises the effort of the Hungarian government in hosting the upcoming Budapest Peace Summit as a constructive contribution to dialogue and stability in Europe; acknowledges that such initiatives demonstrate Hungary's commitment to fostering peace and cooperation within the Union and beyond; therefore considers that the Hungarian Council Presidency's activities should not be interpreted as misconduct, but rather as complementary efforts aligned with the Union's overarching objective of peace and dialogue, fully respecting the Treaties and the role of Member States in foreign policy.'

This is a warning for all of us. The Mayor of Varna was elected by the Bulgarian people to bring reform, transparency and hope. Today, he sits in a high-security cell without credible charges, without fair process, without daylight. He is jailed for what he represents: a new generation that refuses corruption and Kremlin-style power.
This is not about a single politician. This is a European story, and it is a disgrace for us as a free and democratic European Union.
We won't turn a blind eye on Sofia. This is a test. This is a test of our values, of our credibility and of our courage. Because if justice can be twisted for one, it can be twisted for all. And when we stay silent, we become partners in crime.
Colleagues, Europe cannot be strong abroad if it is blind at home. We cannot preach democracy in Kyiv and Tbilisi while ignoring injustice in Sofia. This is the moment to show that Europe still stands for democracy and justice 芒聙聯 everywhere and for everyone.
Let's not fool ourselves. What we witness in Bulgaria happens not by accident. It happens in a country where justice has been captured by oligarchs and where those who stand for reform are treated as enemies of the state. This is the corrupted world of Delyan Peevski and his ally Boyko Borisov. Their despotic system silenced judges, prosecutors, security services, media. Delyan Peevski is a danger for democracy. One by one, he is taking control of the existing political parties. One by one, those political parties are used as tools to silence opponents, while in the past they used to struggle for democracy in the country.
Let me be clear: the dimension is huge. Bulgaria is part of NATO's Black Sea strategy 芒聙聯 a cornerstone of our collective defence. When corruption and political persecution take root there, it weakens Europe's security and gives power to those who wish us to decline.
We urge the European Commission to send an urgent fact-finding mission to Bulgaria to assert the rule of law and the detention of opposition leaders. To enforce conditionality, we call for the suspension of all Recovery and Resilience Facility payments linked to judicial reform until political prisoners are freed and until Bulgaria demonstrates true separation of powers.
Dear colleagues, it is our duty 芒聙聯 as liberal democrats, as Christian democrats, as social democrats 芒聙聯 to intervene. Your parties in the EPP, in the Socialists, might be part of the current government, but your honour and values must prevail over party politics. So move on. Use your European families to apply the necessary pressure. Our duty is to act with clarity, unity and courage. We call for change in Bulgaria now.

For the Council, the rule of law is a constant priority. This is why we have a regular rule of law dialogue, where we examine the specific situation in each Member State.
In this context, Bulgaria will feature in the next discussions and at the General Affairs Council on 17 November, together with three other Member States. There we will look at recent developments in Bulgaria and address challenges and recommendations as identified by the Commission in its annual Rule of Law Report.
These rule of law dialogues facilitate constructive discussions. They help us stay ahead of potential issues and help Member States address rule of law issues. Our democratic institutions face threats from both internal and external sources, polarisation, foreign interference. disinformation, manipulation of elections, to quote just the most concerning ones.
We must find ways to address those challenges together. I am also confident that the upcoming European Democracy Shield will offer us additional tools.

The Commission shares the objectives of the Parliament as regards the promotion and protection of the rule of law. It is essential that all the EU institutions work in close cooperation and coordination to this end. The rule of law report stands as a crucial instrument in advancing our shared objectives.脗聽With the sixth edition of this report, we once again see that it has a real impact across the EU. The report serves as a decisive tool that actively shapes and influences the reforms being implemented in many Member States. Furthermore, the report has proven itself as the foundation for a genuine political dialogue in the rule of law at both European and national level.
First, allow me to delve into the findings and recommendations of the 2025 Rule of Law Report and its chapter dedicated to Bulgaria. The 2025 report notes that Bulgaria has engaged in reform efforts in dialogue with the Commission under the rule of law cycle and the recovery and resilience plan, and there have been several legislative developments, particularly since the beginning of the year.
Starting with the justice system, there has been several such positive developments. A draft law to extend judicial review for prosecutorial decisions, for cases of determination and suspension of investigations for victimless crimes, was tabled. Moreover, the law of a mandatory information meeting on mediation has been adopted, and new digital tools have been introduced to improve access to justice.
However, following the Constitutional Court decision of July 2024, some provisions from the December 2023 comprehensive constitutional reform have been declared unconstitutional, namely the provisions considering unconstitutional and which have been dismissed, including limits on the Prosecutor-General's powers, the mechanism for the effective accountability and criminal liability of the Prosecutor-General and deputies was declared constitutional. Yet, it should be noted that the effectiveness of this mechanism is constrained by some procedural issues.
Furthermore, regarding the Supreme Judicial Council, the Constitutional Court dismissed the overall reform, but considered that a restructuring would be possible. As regards other aspects of the justice system, the long-term secondment of judges and the functioning of the inspectorate through the Supreme Judicial Council remains a concern. As regards the anti-corruption framework, on the positive side, legislation on lobbying transparency is under preparation, and further amendments to the legislation on the protection of whistle-blowers has been entered into force.
More efforts are needed, however, as regards other measures. The report notes that the implementation of the national anti-corruption strategy remains limited. A robust track record of high-level corruption cases is yet to be established. Important gaps remain also in the rules of the integrity of persons with top executive functions. Some steps have been taken to implement the reform of the Commission for Counteracting Corruption and Illegal Assets Forfeiture, but further steps are needed to ensure the effective performance of the Anti-Corruption Commission, in particular to guarantee its independence.
In the context of the Recovery and Resilience Facility, the European Commission found that the milestone related to this reform has not yet been satisfactorily fulfilled. As a result, the Commission proposed a temporary deferred portion of the payment.
As regards media pluralism and media freedom and Bulgaria, concerns regarding the funding and politicisation of the media regulator persists. Furthermore, the draft law aimed at strengthening the independence of the public sector media was not yet adopted, and the appointment of a new director-general of the Bulgarian national television continues to be delayed. While access to public information is improving, it is still hindered by previously existing obstacles, with journalists facing increasing challenges and activities. On the positive side, improvements have been achieved regarding the transparency and allocation of state advertising.
The EU also continues to ensure adequate follow-up by its Member States to the directive, adopted in 2024, following on from the recommendation, to protect journalists and civil society from abusive lawsuits, and they act as a public watchdog. We are supporting Member States to ensure the transposition of the directive by the deadline of 7脗聽May next year. We invite Bulgaria to be ambitious in their transposition and cover also domestic cases in order to enable their courts to adequately handle all SLAPPs and provide safeguards to those targeted with SLAPPs.
The practical implementation of rule of law-making continues to face challenges, and concerns remain regarding the quality of the legislative process. Some independent and regulatory authorities continue to operate with an expired mandate and the renewal processes have been relaunched. On the positive side, attempts to reintroduce legislation required the registration of organisations receiving foreign funding were again dismissed by Parliament.
We follow up on all of these developments and the implementation of the report's recommendations with Bulgaria through close engagements, as we do with all Member States. I look forward to hearing your views.

At the heart of today's debate is the question of proper checks and balances, and, more specifically, of the independence of the judiciary. So, in principle, today's debate shows the importance of the rule of law for all of us. But why do I say 'in principle'? Because exactly that independence has been put into question by some communications.
So let's look into the example at hand: the case of the Mayor of Varna. For that case, the anti-corruption framework and structure is relevant 芒聙聯 which, by the way, was put in place by the previous government. The allegations are about being part of an organised criminal group and about significant bribes in connection to public procurement 芒聙聯 for sure something for the Commission for Anti-Corruption.
Detention has been upheld by five different courts. Although not related to the case, GERB has signalled support for the release from detention. But an independent institution is doing what an independent institution should do: it's investigating, free from political influence.脗聽Having said that, does that mean that there is no room for improvement? I think we should always aim for better.
The 2025 Rule of Law Report describes the task, but for the first time after four elections in a row, we now have a stable government. So, colleagues, let's be really careful with the difference between improving and reforms, and, I quote here, 'the ongoing assaults on institutions'.

Nous, les patriotes pour l'Europe, avec Jordan Bardella, nous affirmons que la v脙漏ritable d脙漏mocratie ne se d脙漏cr脙篓te pas depuis les couloirs de la Commission europ脙漏enne. Let's be clear, pre-trial detention instead of being a measure of last resort is used as a measure by default. A democratically elected mayor is given an ultimatum to choose between switching party allegiance and a new beginning for his mandate, or be arrested. He chose integrity and he is still in custody.
We have an anti-corruption commission, the work programme of which involves only representatives of the opposition, even violating the independence of the National Bank simply to remove from its board an opposition nominee.
We have an acting prosecutor general who clings to his office long after his mandate has expired. Even after the Supreme Court confirmed that he does not have the legal authority to serve. We have an electoral process with countless violations, summed up best by the sincere, 'who gives the money now?', said by a voter immediately after leaving the voting room?
And don't get me started on media freedom and pluralism. It's time for Europe to face these issues head on. The President of the Commission must deliver on her promise that respecting the rule of law should be bound to the access of EU funds.
European parties, too, must hold their own members to the same standards they preach. We cannot afford to repeat the same mistake we made with Orb脙隆n. Nothing fuels the anti-European narrative more than Europe closing its eyes in front of corruption and wrongdoing.

The country had been making progress in the last few years: joining Schengen this January, the euro area in January next year. But now we see that some of the hard-won reforms, particularly for democracy and the rule of law, are being dismantled by the current government.
Blagomir Kotsev, the Mayor of Varna, was arrested in July. To this day, the charges against him remain legally unsubstantiated. What the authorities seem to be holding against him is that he is a pro-European opposition politician in Bulgaria, and that he is a member of a party that wants to fight corruption.脗聽So, when the coerced witness withdrew his statement, that should have been the end of the case, but apparently it is still not 芒聙聯 it keeps on going. How is this possible?
The Commission needs to seriously investigate what's going on with the rule of law, and we need to look into whether the conditions for paying EU funds are still fulfilled.

Se questa Unione europea non fosse solo un bancomat, ma una comunit脙聽 di cittadini che riconosce diritti e valori, allora userebbe ogni strumento legale e diplomatico per cambiare le cose.

The Union has developed a number of instruments to follow and assess developments in this field. Among them, the rule of law report offers a valuable overview of the situation in each Member State, acknowledging That no country is entirely free from challenges.
I am fully confident that Bulgaria, as a member of the European family and as a country deeply attached to our shared values, as shown, among others, through its consistent support for Ukraine in the face of Russian aggression and through membership in Schengen, and soon in eurozone, will continue on its path of commitment to democracy, justice and the rule of law and the full implementation of all recommendations of the European Commission.

The annual rule of law report has become a key preventive instrument to improve the rule of law situation in the EU, including in Bulgaria. However, it is one of the instruments at our disposal. The Commission remains committed to using all the instruments at its disposal to defend the rule of law, in order to provide an effective and proportionate response to any interference with the rule of law. We continue working with national authorities to help them with the implementation of the report recommendation. We also remain fully committed to a dialogue on this matter. The Commission looks forward to continuing to exchange with this Parliament and with the Council, national parliaments and civil society on the annual rule of law reports, including on the country chapters.
More generally, let me also flag two upcoming initiatives of crucial importance to our overall democratic systems. The European democracy shield will put forward actions to promote, protect and strengthen our democracies in the long run. It will be accompanied by a civil society strategy, recognising the key role of civil society organisations in safeguarding and promoting EU values in stronger and more resilient democracies.

I think this year's report is highly relevant as negotiations for the 2028-2034 MFF are under way. I think it offers important lessons and highlights risks that should be considered when shaping future budgets.
The key takeaways from the report: as in previous years, we provide a clean opinion on the reliability of the EU accounts and on the EU's budget revenue. Regarding the regularity of expenditure, we offer two separate opinions: one脗聽for the traditional EU budget and one for the RRF, reflecting the different脗聽nature of these types of spending.
Starting with the traditional EU budget spending, here, for 2024, our estimated error rate is 3.6脗聽%, down from 5.6脗聽% in 2023, meaning it remains material. Given the pervasiveness of the errors identified, we have given an adverse opinion for the sixth consecutive year. The cohesion policy area continues to be the main source that feeds our overall error rate, and it is important to reiterate that for this year, the error rate for cohesion is 5.7脗聽%, down from 9.3脗聽% in 2023. I think in 2023, issues such as pandemic disruptions and time pressure to use 100脗聽% co-financing schemes likely increased irregular spending risks in this area. Consequently, in 2024, the cohesion error rate has returned to levels which were more consistent with previous years.
To summarise our compliance work on the 2024 MFF budget expenditure, while the estimated level of error in spending has decreased, it remains a concern.脗聽I want to make it very clear here today why it is concerning. As I mentioned, the main source of our error stems from cohesion funding. Our audit approach here is based on transactions already reviewed by Member States' audit authorities and/or the Commission, which in theory should be error-free after their checks. Yet we continue to find errors that these auditors should have detected and did not, and this has been an ongoing issue for the last number of years. The persistent failure to detect these errors raises serious questions about the adequacy and effectiveness of oversight and control mechanisms in Member States.
Most concerning is that, in many cases, the same control structures and bodies responsible for cohesion funding are also responsible for checking RRF funds, so similar such errors are likely to present there. However, checks for such errors are not performed when making payments under the RRF. In our view, this lack of oversight undermines assurance and accountability, posing a serious risk to the EU's financial interests.
Indeed, this concern is echoed by EPPO's 2024 annual report, which revealed 307 active funding fraud investigations related to the RRF in EU Member States, making it the third largest in volume across the EU programmes, with cases likely to grow.
It's in this context that we must avoid repeating past mistakes. We must prioritise accountability and transparency as guiding principles in the discussions and development of the next MFF. This is especially important, given that the proposed national plans are very similar in nature and design to the RRF.
Concerning the regularity of the 2024 RRF expenditure, we issued a qualified opinion. As under the RRF, payments are not linked to actual costs, our assessment is limited to the payment conditions set out in the RRF Regulation; that is, that milestones and targets must be satisfactorily fulfilled. Therefore, our opinion for RRF expenditure is not comparable to the one we give for the traditional EU budget expenditure.
This year, we again found milestones and targets linked to payments which were not satisfactorily fulfilled, we noted issues related to the eligibility periods, and we also drew attention to the Court of Justice case concerning two super milestones. We know that there is an increasing trend in the overall financial impact of the quantitative findings. In terms of disbursement, to date, disbursements have totalled EUR脗聽225 billion, just over 60脗聽% of the total funds in terms of grants, with less than a year remaining before the August脗聽2026 deadline. This means significant expenditure still needs to be spent, with nearly 4脗聽200 milestones and targets still to be assessed within just ten months.
Additionally, this year we also published a review on the RRF, consolidating our extensive work on the facility, highlighting the key findings and lessons for future performance-based instruments. We emphasise that while such instruments can be of value, they must only be used when funding links directly to measurable results, with clear traceability to actual costs. It is essential that their design does not compromise accountability.
Looking ahead, our annual report also highlights risks from borrowing for future MFFs, mainly due to the increasing debt obligations of NGEU. By 2027, EU borrowing could exceed EUR脗聽900 billion, nearly ten times the pre-NGEU level. Interest costs could surpass EUR脗聽30 billion for the current MFF 芒聙聯 more than double the initial forecasts. Debt repayments are deferred to future budgets, posing serious challenges to safeguarding those future budgets and ensuring that sufficient resources are available for EU actions. In fact, the proposed 2 trillion budget for 2028-2034 includes an allocation for debt repayment, which could cost up to EUR脗聽31 billion per annum.
Thank you for your attention.

The sound management of the EU budget is our common objective. Of course, I am pleased to note that the court has provided an unqualified opinion on the EU accounts in 2024 that shows that our accounts are complete, transparent and reliable, covering all assets and liabilities, including our borrowing activities.
As in previous years, the court has also concluded that revenue transactions and administrative expenditure remain free from material error. These are positive developments. On the other hand, I acknowledge that the court has maintained its adverse opinion on expenditure and has provided a qualified opinion on the Recovery and Resilience Facility for 2024.
The court's estimated level of error is significantly lower than in 2023 and in 2022, but it is not low enough. Therefore, I want to assure you that the Commission is committed to bringing the level of error down further.
Let me underline that our assessment largely converges with the one from the court. We agree with the court on the spending areas where the risk of errors is high, namely those with complex rules. Some methodological discrepancies with the ECA's assessment remain. Nonetheless, we largely agree with the court on the root causes of errors identified.脗聽We will continue to work with the court on fostering common understanding and reduce divergences in views.
Ladies and gentlemen, the Commission is committed to bring the level of error down further and to implement the recommendations of the court. They help us to improve the Commission's management and control systems. Let me conclude by saying that the Commission has accepted or partially accepted all but one of the court's recommendations expressed in this year's report, and that we also take seriously into account your views with respect to the next Multiannual Financial Framework.
