But when it comes to the RRF, I believe the Court of Auditors should be playing a much stronger role. The RRF is a real symbol of solidarity, but the court has identified gaps in transparency, cost verification and control consistency right across our Member States and this makes it difficult to show value for money and increases the risk of duplication with other EU funds.
So, for credibility of EU spending, I am looking for the Commissioner and the Commission to develop a harmonised framework for reporting and verifying the actual costs of RRF-funded measures, to establish a central EU registry, to track projects right across the EU institutions and prevent duplication of funding, to strengthen the ex-post audit and clawback mechanisms to ensure proper use of funds, and finally to publish a clear, accessible data-linking funding to tangible results that citizens can understand and trust.

I would like to have two questions. Has the Court identified cases where the Climate Pact investments did not deliver measurable environmental benefits? If so, how significant were these discrepancies relative to the total reported climate spending?
And the second question is on electro-mobility. To which extent did the Court elaborate and consider the life-cycle emissions of electric vehicles, including production and energy sourcing, within the audit scope, particularly when attributing emission reductions to electric vehicles' uptake?

The report that most honourable President Murphy has presented is actually partially a ready indictment for a prosecutor. President Murphy should be probably appointed to the European Union's bankruptcy trustee, which I would recommend because he himself is probably one of the most reliable clerks we have in this otherwise criminal organisation 芒聙聯 because had that happened to some private business, some manager losing that much money, losing trace of that much money, that would be a case for ...
(The President cut off the speaker)

One of you said, 'We have to learn from our mistakes and not repeat them'. I could not agree more. Most of the comments were about the mistakes related to the RRF, and I will come back to it because, indeed, clarifications are needed in what extent the proposal of the Commission learns the lesson from the RRF mistakes. But we should not forget about, and the report of the Court is also about, other spending programmes, I should even say primarily about spending programmes other than the RRF. When you look into the report, that is what it is.
So what are those lessons that we should learn? The Court supports, but also our assessment identifies, that the areas with complex rules are those areas where the risk of errors is the highest. Incidentally, in those areas payments are also based on costs incurred by, and declared by, beneficiaries. We talk here about cohesion policy, we talk here about the eco-schemes under the common agricultural policy. Just two examples, but I think very much prominent also in the report of the Courts.
So what lesson should we learn? That is reflected in our proposal for the next MFF: we reduce the number of spending programmes 芒聙聯 and that should be one of the key lessons. And let us take the example of the cohesion policy family. We have the Cohesion Fund, we have the European Regional Development Fund, we have European Social Fund Plus, we have the Just Transition Fund, we have the Modernisation Fund, we have Interreg, with separate legal bases, with different eligibility criteria, with different time frames of implementation. That is the reason for the errors. Therefore, we propose a single rule book and we propose the concentration of the spending programmes.
Now, RRF. Yes, we know that we need to learn the lessons from 芒聙聯 and I will give you only a few examples 芒聙聯 lack or insufficient transparency, that has been mentioned also in this debate. What do we do? We make sure that in the next MFF, information on beneficiaries, not only final beneficiaries 芒聙聯 and as you know, the debate is still ongoing with the Member States on who is a final beneficiary 芒聙聯 but also on recipients, final recipients, contractors and subcontractors,脗聽should be publicly available, both at the level of the Member States and also at the level of the Union. And transparency is the most powerful tool to ensure that the European taxpayers' money is spent properly.
Another criticism: insufficient protection of the EU's financial interests, again present in this debate. What do we propose for the future? We propose 芒聙聯 which is missing in the current areas 芒聙聯 that the state aid and public procurement rules should be part of the Member States' key requirements, and there, they should be subject to audits.
We are also aware, and that was present in today's debate, about the weaknesses of the delivery model. Weaknesses related primarily to the question of costs. Therefore, we propose also comprehensive costing review at midterm and confirmation by Member States at the end of the period that payments to Member States did not exceed payments to beneficiaries. The Commission will also have the right to propose amendments if we disagree with the assessment of the Member States.
We are also aware that there are weaknesses of the Member States' management and control systems. And actually, the model which we propose gives us a chance to, at the inception of the national plan, correct those deficiencies and to make meeting the 15 requirements defined in the regulation a prerequisite of entering into contractual obligation in the next multiannual financial framework.

I think it's normal that we don't always agree on everything, and I think there's nothing wrong with that. I think it should be remembered, though, as I think the Commissioner pointed out in his earlier presentation, that we have a common goal here, and the common goal is to improve the financial management and to protect the financial interests of the EU budget.
It is a collective effort, I think, based on our specific mandates. I am very happy that you say our recommendations are practically all accepted. That's good. It relates to one which was raised by Mr脗聽Erixon about the access to documents. We actually have a recommendation in Chapter 8 on migration, border management, security and defence about the access to documents which you have partly accepted.脗聽So there is something there.
But I really hope that you listen to our concerns on the RRF. I don't want to pre-empt their opinions, but based on your contribution here, I don't think we will still see eye to eye based on what you said. I have to say up front, because there's no point saying after the event when it's too late.
I think we're facing a very challenging period ahead. Look at the amount of money: we still have 40脗聽% of these RRF funds which have to be spent by August脗聽2026. We have an MFF in 21-27 where the absorption rate is 7脗聽% at the moment 芒聙聯 5脗聽% for cohesion. For us, based on our past experience, the worst thing you can do is put pressure on Member States to spend money by a certain deadline, because there is a distinct correlation between the errors we will find and that situation. I think that's to be avoided at all costs if it can be.
I want to take this opportunity to reiterate our concerns regarding the delivery and governance of the RRF, as well as the existing control mechanisms within the traditional budget. If these issues are not addressed, the transparency and traceability risks we have identified could significantly increase for the MFF. I can only recommend that you look at our RRF review, which we published on 6脗聽May. There's a summary page there. It's one page, and I think it outlines in simple language the key concerns and challenges we see with the RRF.
The Commissioner mentioned in his summary that we shouldn't focus too much on the RRF because it's not the substantial part of the budget in monetary terms. We're talking about EUR脗聽200脗聽billion compared to maybe EUR脗聽56脗聽billion for the RRF for last year. I think that's not the issue. Given the political fanfare which went with the RRF, the reputational risk with the RRF is huge for the EU project as a whole. I think it's not just a question of the actual proportional amount of money, it's about the expectations that have been generated in the EU and across the Member States.
I don't want to pre-empt. We're working on our opinions, which we will provide on the MFF proposals, and these will highlight the risks and lessons learned based on our extensive experience. Again, going back to reiterate, because I think it's very important what you said, the objective of these opinions is that we don't repeat the past mistakes. We're not in a crisis situation now, as was the case when the RRF was designed. So we have time to think about this and make sure that it's a better-designed regulation and a better-designed delivery mechanism.
As I say, we have a lot of concerns. I don't want to pre-empt our opinion on the national plans, but as I say, from what you say already, I'm not so convinced that all of the concerns that we have been addressed to our full satisfaction.
With that, I'd like to thank the Members here today who thanked the Court for the work. I mean, this work is not possible without the dedication of our staff. I would like to thank all the staff who were involved in this product and the reporting members and the college for producing a document which I think is high quality and I think is an important contribution to your work here and into the decision-making process, particularly given the circumstances we are in at the moment.

We welcome all efforts to achieve a just and lasting peace, and we support the US in its efforts. However, we have not yet seen any indications that Putin is generally ready for peace. On the contrary, Russia is escalating its war in Ukraine ahead of the winter season. We see more attacks against civilians and the energy infrastructure.
The Council has been clear: the EU stands united in its support of Ukraine's sovereignty and right to determine its own future. Without Ukraine, there cannot be negotiations about Ukraine. We must stay united and increase our support to Ukraine. This also includes diplomatic efforts, and we must continue to increase the pressure on Russia: economic pressure, military pressure, political pressure. The goal is that Russia engages truly in negotiations.
Sanctions are an essential part of the EU's policy to achieve this objective. This week we aim to adopt the 19th sanctions package. This package will target the Russian fossil fuels, the shadow fleet and the circumventions. And we already aim for a for next package. The diplomatic talks have confirmed over and over again that nobody wants peace more than the Ukrainians themselves. This peace should start with a full, unconditional and instant ceasefire at the first step. The Council has supported efforts for a ceasefire and leaders will discuss recent efforts at tomorrow's European Council meeting.
Last but not least, let me recall that the EU accession process is still the most important security guarantee that the EU can give to Ukraine. Accession is essential for Ukraine's stability, resilience and future security.

The fundamentals have not changed. Ukraine is ready for a ceasefire, but Russia shows no genuine interest in peace. We all support President Trump's efforts to end this war, but let us be clear. Putin will only negotiate when he understands that he is losing this war. The European Commission, together with all EU institutions and the Member States, has mobilised unprecedented support for Ukraine, especially because Ukraine needs our continued support on every front: military support to defend its people and territory, financial resilience to keep hospitals, schools and public services running, protection of the energy infrastructure constantly under Russian attack, humanitarian assistance to for millions of its citizens in need at home and across Europe, uninterrupted train through the solidarity lanes to stay connected to global markets.脗聽We are also supporting Ukraine to implement ambitious reforms towards EU membership, which also strengthen the country's institutional resilience.
From the very beginning, the European Commission acted swiftly, decisively, and in unity with our Member States. Together, we have provided EUR 177.5 billion of assistance, including EUR 63.2 billion of military support. Together, we are the largest donor, the largest public investor and the strongest partner in Ukraine's defence. With the coalition of the willing, we are shaping the EU's contribution to a robust and credible security guarantees, building on the 2024 joint security commitments.
Dear colleagues, we have come a long way in supporting Ukraine, yet we need to do more. Now is the time to accelerate our collective efforts to help Ukraine stand strong, not only to defend itself, but also to achieve just and lasting peace when hostilities give way to negotiations.
First, we must keep up our military support. We have two important missions on the ground: the EU Military Assistance Mission, that has already trained 8080 2000 Ukrainian soldiers, and the European Union's Advisory Mission, our civilian mission, that supports Ukraine, law enforcement and justice.脗聽We are also scaling up our defence industry cooperation with Ukraine.
Second, we must maintain our predictable long term financial assistance for Ukraine's defence, reconstruction and resilience. The new reparation loans proposed by the European Commission, funded from the cash balance associated with the immobilised Russian assets, is indispensable.
Third, we should make the most out of Ukraine facility to ensure micro and macro financial stability and support Ukraine's reforms.
And fourth, we must accelerate the EU accession process. Our most important security guarantee to Ukraine. Our commitment to stability, resilience and shared economic opportunity for Ukrainians as well as for European citizens. Ukraine has achieved remarkable reform progress in extremely challenging circumstances. And it is time to open negotiations on Cluster 1, the fundamentals for accession.
Our message is clear. We will stand with Ukraine for as long as it takes and for as long as it matters.

But while Putin has always had the same maximalist positions, which are all unacceptable and infamous, even when it comes to the alleged need for 'denazification' that he always talks about, I think the Nazis here in the House support him. It cannot be about the denazification of Ukraine, with a Jewish President at the top.
The other unfortunate thing is that on the American side we see an almost daily shift of positioning. That requires, finally, that we really also get our acts together when it comes to the peace project 芒聙聯 not only the ongoing programmes, but there is talk about an alleged 12-point plan that is being worked on with Ukraine that is right and good 芒聙聯 so we find a way forward to strengthen Ukraine's position for the time when it comes. Well, the American positioning changed so quickly that even our title with a reference to Budapest is outdated.
On the substance, it's right that there is no such thing. It was, as such, already an unfriendly act, be it initiated from the Russians or the Americans, to hold it in Budapest. They should have done it in in T脙录rkiye, in Istanbul, where we have had previous rounds of such talks.
But the real point is, in my mind, that we need to make it very clear that if the ceasefire is the first point where apparently the United States President is now back to this position, ceasefire first and then negotiations, it must be something that Ukraine can live with. And that means that we will never accept any legal transfer of Ukrainian territory to Russia. De facto, something can temporarily be accepted, but never in a position that we would legally agree to it.

Putin is still not interested in peace 芒聙聯 surprise, surprise. Ukraine wants peace. We want peace. Real peace involves talks and compromises, yes.脗聽The US could play a critical role, but we can do without Witkoff's ignorance, incompetence and conflicts of interest.
When Putin thinks he is gaining ground and gets to keep it, he will continue. Putin will not de-escalate unless Europe and the United States firmly and decisively escalate support and pressure. So Europe must take the initiative as a credible, proactive force for peace and not as a background actor for a Trump-Putin tragedy.
My proposal: enable deep strikes, boost Ukraine's air defence now, adopt a reparations loan and fair talks framework, starting with a ceasefire but including real security guarantees, accountability, reparations, the children, the prisoners 芒聙聯 not just territory. Act, Member States, act to defend Ukraine and Europe!

Let us state it clearly: so-called peace dictated by Moscow would not be peace at all. It would reward an aggressor, legitimise occupation and betray the Ukrainian people who fight and die for their freedom. A just and durable peace must therefore be concluded on Ukraine's terms, respecting its sovereignty, territorial integrity and the right of its citizens to decide their future.
We will stand with Ukraine until victory. That means military support sufficient to repel aggression, financial support to sustain the state, and a shared plan for reconstruction based on accountability, including the use of Russian frozen assets.
Let us therefore send a clear message before any summit that might happen in the coming weeks. Europe will not surrender to blackmail, will not normalise aggression and will not abandon Ukraine. Only a peace grounded in justice can endure.

Therefore, he has playtime with President Trump. We saw it in Alaska. We see it clearly now in this very slow preparation for a possible Budapest conference. The panic in the Kremlin when Trump talked about giving Tomahawk cruise missiles to Ukraine shows that only the threat of Western military and economic force will put fear to Putin and force him into negotiations.
We can't trust in President Trump in this crucial issue; he is far too easily fooled by Russians. Therefore, we Europeans need to send a clear message to Putin: regardless of what the USA decides, we will increase sanctions on Russia and increase funding and military support to Ukraine, including capability to long-range strikes. And we need to actually do it, not just give declarations and talk about it. It is our responsibility, and it is in our interests. Europe is safe when Ukraine is safe.
Slava Ukraini!

At the same time, the EU supports the Russian criminal regime by buying Russian energy. War criminal Putin fools around and continues killing. President Trump continues changing his positions. That is reality.
We are the last hope of Ukrainians. Together with the 'coalition of the willing' we have to get our act together. We must support Ukraine until full victory over aggressor in order to bring fair and durable peace to Ukraine and Europe. We must give Ukraine much needed military support, fully implement sanctions, stop the Russian shadow fleet, start using Russian frozen assets, stop financing the Russian war economy and bring the aggressor to justice. And we should do it in solidarity.
Thank you,脗聽Slava Ukraini.

We must continue to quickly handle climate change on two fronts: mitigation and adaptation. The EU has a strong track record in both areas, with ambitious policies to reduce its greenhouse emissions, prevent and reduce climate-related risk. We especially must ensure that Europe becomes more resilient against climate-related water extremes like floods.
On 21 October, the Council approved conclusions on water resilience. The Council stressed the need to strengthen water infrastructure, security and real-time early warning systems for floods. Also, the Council underlined the importance of cooperation and coordination between borders. We must also strengthen the EU's preparedness, resilience and capacity to respond to crises and disasters, to tackle the changing landscape of risks and threats, such as extreme weather. These are priorities for the Danish Presidency. This is why we aim to make progress with the legislative proposal to revise the Union Civil Protection Mechanism.

At the same time, I would like to pay tribute to all those who stepped up to help during and after the floods.
In the early hours of 29 October 2024, a cold drop hit eastern Spain. Torrential rains poured down more than 700mm in just 12 hours. Rivers burst their banks across the Mediterranean coast and flash floods swept through Valencia. It was an unprecedented disaster, and all the data tells us events like this will happen more often.
Like many of you, we were deeply shocked by the images. People being rescued from rooftops, cars piled up, roads buried in mud. Whole communities devastated right here in Europe.
Honourable colleagues, from the very start, the European Union stood in full solidarity with Spain, its authorities and all those affected. We helped support relief efforts and speed up recovery in the affected areas. The Emergency Response Coordination Centre, 24/7 heart of the EU Civil Protection Mechanism, stayed in constant contact with Spanish civil protection teams ready to assist.
On 29 October 2024, Spain activated the Copernicus Rapid Mapping Service to assess the flood and the damages. A few days later, on 8 November, Spain requested help through the EU Civil Protection Mechanism. Several Member States offered assistance. Machinery and vehicles were quickly sent to the scene, and two EU liaison officers were deployed to help coordinate operations on the ground. This was European solidarity in action.
Just a few weeks ago on 6 October 2025, early warning systems again signalled heavy rain in Valencia. Spain, and the ERCC were again immediately in contact in full preparedness mode to assess the situation and discuss possible measures. That is what seamless coordination between our civil protection authorities looks like. It also shows why civil protection is a cornerstone of Europe's security and essential to protecting our people and our communities.
But with this sad anniversary is also a reminder, we must be better prepared. We must anticipate these events, prepare together and support each other when disaster strikes. And that is why the Commission has adopted the Preparedness Union strategy, and we are already putting the actions into practice. This is the EU's first comprehensive preparedness strategy across all policies, all levels of government and all parts of society. It is about changing the way we work and adopting a true mindset of preparedness.
The strategy takes an all hazard approach, covering every kind of natural or manmade risk, from floods to cascading effects. It brings together all relevant policies and actors under one common goal, a whole of government and a whole of society effort. Floods are a clear example. EU civil protection support is becoming脗聽even more important.
But response alone is no longer enough. And that is why many of the key actions in this strategy focus directly on flood preparedness and on tackling environmental risks before they escalate.
Here are five key areas. First, preparedness by design. From now on, preparedness will be built in all EU laws, policies and programmes, not added as an afterthought. This must go hand in hand with flexible financial framework as proposed under the new Multiannual Financial Framework. For floods, this means investing in better preparedness through planning, environmental protection, smarter agriculture, cohesion, funding and public awareness. Only by connecting these areas can we build a truly integrated approach to flood preparedness.
Second, stronger foresight and preparedness. By combining solid national systems with closer European cooperation we can better protect our people and our ecosystem from ever more frequent and intense disasters.
Third, strengthening our early warning systems, we will strengthen the use of Copernicus to track natural hazards, including floods. And together with Member States, we will develop guidelines to help communicate with the public before and during grave crisis.
Fourth, raising public awareness. Too often people simply do not know how to prepare. That is why the strategy includes concrete measures to boost populations' preparedness, including guidelines for Member States to ensure citizens can be self-sufficient for at least 72 hours.
And fifth, acting earlier, faster and better together. The Commission will create a new EU crisis coordination hub, building on the expertise of our Emergency Response Coordination Centre to improve coordination when crises hit and manage their cross-sectoral impacts. We will also step up cooperation with science and the private sector to develop innovative solutions that help us to prevent, prepare for and respond to the growing risk of flooding floodings.
Honourable Members, our Water Resilience Strategy, adopted by the Commission in June 2025, is one of the key actions under the Preparedness Union Strategy. It calls for the building of resilience of floods, droughts and also other extreme weathers proactively and in solidarity across borders. The goal is simple to help Member States better identify, risk and take prevention actions by protecting and restoring ecosystems. It will use nature-based solutions to slow water flows and store it in our soil and our forests. This strategy also ensures proper funding for both green and grey infrastructure, for smarter water management and stronger flood protection.
The Commission is also supporting flood risk prevention and preparedness through the Cohesion Policy Fund. Already in the 2021 to 2027 period, EUR 7 billion of EU funds are available for Member States to invest in the prevention and management of floods. Moreover, cohesion policy funds can now quickly and flexibly be mobilised to address post-disaster recovery and enhance disaster resilience through the Restore initiative, which is already supporting the reconstruction after the DANA floods in Valencia.
Additionally, in the ongoing mid-term review of the cohesion policy, Member states are encouraged to increase the allocation in flood-risk management and climate adaptation. Redirecting resources to these actions provides greater flexibility and incentives to the authorities to accelerate implementation of prevention and preparedness projects on the ground, and we will reinforce the European solidarity to help Member States recover from water related disasters.
At the start of this month. On 3 October, the Commission proposed to mobilise EUR 946 million from the European Solidarity Fund to help Spain recover from the devastating DANA floods. This comes after the EUR 100 million already paid in advance to help kick start recovery efforts. This helps restoring infrastructure, providing temporary accommodation, funding rescue services and cleaning up and secure, preventing infrastructure and protecting cultural heritage. And yesterday, the commission mobilised EUR 166 million from the European Social Fund Plus to support self-employed workers who lost their businesses, provide training for unemployed and young people and strengthening counselling support for students in the affected areas.
Beyond these strategies, the Commission has also presented a proposal to upgrade the Union's civil protection mechanism. This mechanism, delivered during DANA just as it did during this summer, wildfires during COVID, and it continues to do so in supporting those affected by Russia's war against Ukraine.
The proposed revision builds on the lessons learned from DANA. It is ambitious but realistic, designed to strengthen Europe's response capacity, improve anticipation and promote a more integrated, cross-sectoral approach. It will help Member States to respond faster in crisis and involve all actors who can add value, including when needed, the military.
The rapporteurs were appointed this month, and I count on the European Parliament to move swiftly on this important file.
So, honourable Members, the losses caused by DANA remind us that we must go further and ensure that next time disaster strikes, Europe is better prepared. We owe it to the victims, to their families, and to all of those that risk their lives in every disaster to build a Europe that is ready, resilient and united.

Let's be honest, dear colleagues, Europe could be drowning and burning at the same time. Europe is not prepared. Europe is not adapting to the changing climate. As the rapporteur on European water resilience, I speak on behalf of all Europeans, especially those most vulnerable, those who adapt every single day to survive and to live with dignity at the frontline of climate disaster.
We must be prepared when the next disaster strikes, and that means investing in water systems that store, reuse and protect. That means making health preparedness a cornerstone of our climate response. That means mapping risks across borders and funding prevention.
Climate resilience must now become Europe's new growth model, anchored in prevention, powered by innovation and guided by solidarity. Because climate resilience is not about resisting climate and climate change, it is about shaping it together before it reshapes how we are today.

Let me assure you that the Commission is strongly committed to protecting information, staff and networks from illicit Intelligence gathering actions, the Commission has in place a robust security framework with rules and processes aimed at identifying and mitigating risks, as well as strengthening the security resilience of our staff.
A key role in this respect plays a specialised team responsible for human and digital counterintelligence analysis. On intelligence gathering, we rely on a significant level of cooperation with the services of the Member States and other EU institutions.
To strengthen the resilience of our staff regarding human and digital espionage, we carry out awareness raising campaigns. Through these, staff members are reminded regularly how to recognise and deal with intelligence gathering attempts. But we also follow closely the media reports. Allegations of espionage by the Hungarian authorities against staff of the EU institutions are taken by the Commission services very seriously.
Regarding the recent allegations, I can confirm that there is an internal investigation ongoing for operational reasons and to ensure the integrity of the investigation, we must ensure the confidentiality of the process and hence I cannot communicate on the details. But I want to make it clear that the Commission services are closely examining the case.
We also recognise the strong and legitimate interest of the Parliament and EU citizens in this matter. As I emphasised in another case related to Olaf officials operating in Hungary, ensuring the right conditions for the staff to carry out their mission and looking after their safety is and will remain our important task, also, for me personally.
I want to assure you that the President of the Commission also takes this matter very seriously. These alleged incidents undermine the principle of loyal cooperation between Member States and EU institutions. Even though the investigations have not yet been fully completed, I can assure you that the Commission will raise its concerns with the Hungarian Government at all levels. We are fully committed to devote continued efforts to protect the security of the Commission.

But if there was no espionage, there should be, ladies and gentlemen, because you are the enemy. You are the enemy. And no surprise for the AfD, like Mr Jungbluth, the people behind all this are people of Jewish descent once again. What we are seeing right now is nothing more than treachery. Treachery against Europe. Treachery against the European Union. Treachery against the people of Hungary. Because the people of Hungary democratically decided to be part of the European Union and to build together with us the European idea.

芒聙聯 die Anfrage zur m脙录ndlichen Beantwortung an den Rat 脙录ber die Klimakonferenz 2025 der Vereinten Nationen (COP脗聽30) in Bel脙漏m (Brasilien) von Antonio Decaro, L脙颅dia Pereira, Annalisa Corrado, Emma Wiesner, P脙陇r Holmgren, Catarina Martins im Namen des Ausschusses f脙录r Umwelt, Klima und Lebensmittelsicherheit (O-000028/2025 芒聙聯 B10-0011/2025) (2025/2666(RSP)) und.
芒聙聯 die Anfrage zur m脙录ndlichen Beantwortung an die Kommission 脙录ber die Klimakonferenz 2025 der Vereinten Nationen (COP脗聽30) in Bel脙漏m (Brasilien) von Antonio Decaro, L脙颅dia Pereira, Annalisa Corrado, Emma Wiesner, Catarina Martins, P脙陇r Holmgren im Namen des Ausschusses f脙录r Umwelt, Klima und Lebensmittelsicherheit (O-000029/2025 芒聙聯 B10-0012/2025) (2025/2666(RSP)).

'Minister Bjerre, Commissioner Hoekstra, dear colleagues, to achieve an outcome from the UN Climate Change Conference (COP30) that meaningfully advances the agenda on mitigation, adaptation, finance and loss and damage, the EU needs to strengthen its climate diplomacy. What actions are the Commission and the Council taking individually to ensure that the COP30 in Bel脙漏m concludes with significant progress, particularly in relation to the following three points:
1. raising the collective ambition in nationally determined contributions, in line with the goals of the Paris Agreement;
2. mobilising climate finance by the public and private sectors towards the goals agreed at COP29, including through innovative financing schemes and equitable burden-sharing mechanisms;
3. implementing and further strengthening the commitments and actions agreed in the first global stocktake, which forms part of the UAE Consensus?'
Commissioner, Council, colleagues, the climate conference lays ahead. In less than a month from now, we hope to reach a conclusion that will deliver on climate neutrality in 2050, on climate finance, on ending fossil fuel subsidies, on climate adaptation, indigenous rights, improving biodiversity and reducing greenhouse gases massively.
Parliament's draft motion for a resolution calls for the Union to lead by example and underlines the need for a science-based EU climate target for 2030. It highlights the triple planetary crisis of climate, biodiversity and pollution because these are interlinked and we need an integrated response. Importantly, it recognises that climate change is a threat multiplier. Climate diplomacy is building stability and building peace. I hope that when the resolution is adopted, the Commissioner and the Council feel that the Parliament stands ready to report a great outcome at the climate conference.
As global warming accelerates, we have no time to hesitate because we are reaching a scale of climate consequences which almost seems unimaginable. The Commissioner himself recently warned us of the collapse of the Gulf Stream. He rightfully called it a wake-up call that would start an era where we cannot prepare for what will come. With that news came just recently a scientific discovery on the coral reefs. They are close to almost irreversibly dying off, while more than a billion people depend on healthy corals. Just imagine these people seeking refuge elsewhere, as they rightfully would.
Our farmers already face increasing droughts, which causes more and more crop losses, not only here, but across the world. Harvests of coffee and cacao, but also close to home, grapes are noticeably delivering less and less. People feel this directly. Prices are rising.
There are no quick fixes. There is no clever trick or path to become climate-neutral. It sure will be hard. It tests our society's capacity to innovate, to adapt, to stay at course. Because each compromise on climate, each delay on environment will hit us like a boomerang. Stalling our efforts now would only mean accelerating the climate crisis. There is no scenario where the cost of actions outweighs those of inaction, and action delivers not only for climate but also for our economy. Decarbonising and competitiveness go hand in hand. It offers sustainable growth in every sense of the word.
We have to address the elephant in the room. We must avoid that Europe shows up empty-handed, and I have to say, I have a lot of trust in this Council, the Presidency of Denmark, and this Commissioner. Because what will we tell those island states that are shrinking year by year beneath rising seas, to countries facing droughts that destroy livelihoods, to the regions overwhelmed by floods, without the means to prevent such catastrophes? I'm afraid that we will disappoint them while we know all too well what lies ahead because their present is our future, and our future is not that far away. Today we remember the DANA floods in Spain from last year. This summer, wildfires raged through Portugal, while in July, heatwaves claimed hundreds of lives in Romania, Bulgaria, Greece and Cyprus alone. There would not be a more cynical time for Europe to arrive at the climate conference empty-handed.
But again, with this Commissioner, I have confidence that it will not happen 芒聙聯 because not so long ago, we led, we guided, we inspired and we united, we built alliances with countries and shared our ambition. We show that climate action works by bringing down emissions in record time. This is not the time to stall our efforts.

The Paris Agreement has made a difference. The agreement has pushed global climate action by calling on parties to set their climate targets, advance in adaption plans and mobilise climate finance. The projections for global temperature rise are now lower than before the Paris Agreement, but reaching the 1.4-degree goal of the agreement will be difficult. COP30 should be a key milestone in our efforts to that end.
The Council outlined its overall position for COP30 in Council conclusions, which were approved yesterday. Allow me to underline our main priorities. The EU believes that the COP should address the global level of climate ambition. According to the Paris Agreement, parties are expected to submit their new climate commitments ahead of COP30, the so-called nationally determined contributions (NDCs).
It is likely that there will be a gap in the ambitions to reach the 1.5-degree goal. It is important that COP30 provides a clear response to the NDCs, addressing the level of ambition and implementation.
The EU's position is that this would require a dedicated political space, a negotiated outcome that addresses the collective level of ambition and implementation. This is our clear call to the Brazilian COP Presidency, which is currently considering how to address the topic.
As the Presidency, we are also firmly committed to working towards a submission of the next EU NDCs ahead of COP30.
Linked to the priority of climate ambition is the implementation of the commitments agreed within the first global stocktake at COP28. We agreed to transition away from fossil fuels, to treble renewable energy capability and to double the annual rate of improvements in energy efficiency.
In its outreach ahead of the COP, the EU has stressed the need to collectively implement all aspects of the first global stocktake.
Climate finance is another aspect raised in the oral question. More climate finance is needed to respond to the increasing impacts of climate change. After the agreement on the new climate finance goal of COP29, we expect the discussions this year to focus on a call for scaling the broader climate finance to USD脗聽1.3脗聽trillion annually by 2035.
The EU will approach these discussions in line with its position as a leading contributor of climate finance.脗聽In 2023, the EU provided EUR脗聽28.6脗聽billion in public climate finance. We also mobilised EUR脗聽7.2脗聽billion from the private sector.
The EU is committed to remaining a trusted partner in support of the countries most vulnerable to climate change. But the public finance from developed countries cannot and should not fill the gap alone. We need a broader range of contributors, more private finance and fewer barriers to financial flows.
A key deliverable for the Brazilian Presidency is progress on the global goal on adaption that was introduced by the Paris Agreement. COP30 is expected to adopt indicators to guide adoption action, identify challenges and capture successes at global and national level.
In line with these priorities, the Presidency, Member States and the Commission are working hard to shape the result of the COP30, and the European Parliament is an active participant in framing the EU priorities for the COP.

Unfortunately, Europe is very much at the receiving end of these horrible events. But with whomever you talk from wherever across the globe, these type of events are taking place everywhere and their frequency is going up 芒聙聭 I was slightly disturbed by a sign that went up over there -, it is going up year by year, unfortunately.
The first the first thing Mr Chahim asked, and I think there was there is there is also a very important point, is how do we navigate in this hugely complex geopolitical world and how do we continue to engage with others? And it is true, every single COP I have been involved with, we have stressed how dramatically difficult the geopolitics of the time were. And still, if you think back about last year or the year before, it is fair to say that things didn't get any easier. An interlocutor I talked to recently said to me when I asked, well, how are things? He basically said, well, worse than last year, but better than next year. And that is pretty much the geopolitical situation we're in.
But there is no alternative than to continue to engage. And we are doing it, of course, with those very close to us like the British, the Australians, the Canadians, the South Koreans and many others who are very much in the same position as we are. We've also made huge efforts again and for all the right reasons, with our friends from the cities, most recently in New York with our friends and partners from Africa. Just recently, when I met many of them in New York, last week in Brazil, but also in August in Spain, when we had a very good conference with the Minister of India, who I had the pleasure to meet last week in Brazil, but also earlier this year in New Delhi itself. And of course, with China, where we were with a number of Commissioners earlier this year, and who we also had over in Brussels, and with whom we're going to have the MOCHA conference in just a couple of weeks.
So a lot of diplomacy that is going on because there is a lot at stake. I wanted to thank Parliament for the interaction we've already had, and I also wanted to thank you for the COP 30 resolution and for your call to continue to lead in international climate negotiations.
And leadership is very much about what you do and what others can expect of you. But let me stress once again that leadership is also very much about what you articulate, about what you expect from others.脗聽And it is important that we also bring the message forward that with the type of world we're living in, solidarity and reciprocity do need to go hand in hand.
We're ten years on from the Paris Agreement. On the one hand, we're very much delivering, and at the same time we are not delivering enough. Yes, we are on track to 2.1 degrees and we must collectively accelerate action to keep the global temperature increase below 1.5. And that is why we need to deliver new, ambitious, economy-wide national determined contributions ahead of COP 30.
Now, as the minister just said, the Energy Council yesterday adopted a set of conclusions that 芒聙聭 just to be upfront about it 芒聙聭 I very much like and subscribe to.
First, an outcome that acknowledges the findings of the UNFCCC and the IPCC Synthesis Report. And being clear about the ambition and the need to address the gap.
Secondly, on agreeing on the modalities of the UE dialogue, to follow up on the implementation of the first global stocktake.
Third, the fact that we are deciding on a way forward of the Sharm el-Sheikh dialogue on Article 2.1(c) of the Paris Agreement.
Fourth, the capturing in the Baku to Bel脙漏m Roadmap of the 1.3 trillion. Hugely important, particularly in making sure that we bring others along.
Fifth, an agreement on the UAE芒聙聯Bel脙漏m Work Programme on adaptation indicators, because the problem has mushroomed into something that is so large that mitigation alone, unfortunately no longer will do the trick.
And sixth and finally, enhancing capacity to implement a just transition domestically.
That is a lot. And I'm not going to hide the fact that this is going to be complicated once again. Around 100 countries and then talking about NDC accounting for nearly 80脗聽% of global emissions, have submitted or announced their post-2030 climate goals. But despite that, we expect a level of collective ambition to be insufficient, insufficient to keep 1.5 degrees within reach. And that has very, very significant ramifications for everyone across the globe.
So we need a decision. And that is one of the main parameters we have to face in Bel脙漏m, we need a decision on how to address ambition, implementation and implementation gaps, barriers and also opportunities. And we do need collective agreement and collective commitment to step up efforts in the near term.
Secondly, on the GST, forgive me for all the horrible abbreviations, but it is meaningful stuff that lies beneath it. We do like and we do ask for all aspects of the GST to be implemented in the relevant UNFCCC work streams. This is not a supermarket. You cannot have some of the elements we agreed on without the others. It is the full to do list for everyone involved. It's that simple.
Third, the NC QG, which basically means the money. Here, it is hugely important that the difficult agreement we managed to reach last year, we put it into practice and countries like the European Union basically live up to their commitments. They make it concrete, they make it actionable. They make it plannable, particularly for those in the Global South who desperately wait for the money to arrive and to be able to do the things they need to do with it.
Just to give you the numbers again, because it shows how the European Union is leading this effort. In 2023, we delivered roughly 30 billion in public climate funds, on top of the 7.2 billion mobilised from the private sector. And that makes us by far, by far the continent that takes the most responsibility. And I agree we should. But it is also clear that we do need others to join the party and to help out, because the European Union alone cannot solve this problem.
And then, I've mentioned that earlier today, to the esteemed Members of this Parliament as well, there is a huge opportunity hidden in the domain of carbon pricing that might not be part of the major negotiations, but it is a topic for which the time has come, and many of our friends globally have embarked on looking into it or already agreeing on it. And sometimes it's very similar to our ETS system, something that is a bit farther out. But pricing carbon is probably one of the most effective things that humanity can do to come up with a solution. And interestingly enough, interestingly enough, I think that is even true here to some degree, across the political spectrum, in many geographies, there is appetite for such a type of tool, and that is simply very good news.
So forgive me for being a bit long Chair, but this is going to be important. We have a lot of work to do ahead of the COP at the COP, and certainly after the COP, because then again, implementation really needs to take place. But we will give it our best. And we do invite nations from all over the globe, whether they're developed or developing, whether they have done a lot or not that much to join in and make sure we do tackle one of the largest problems humanity is currently facing.

Our next climate conference is approaching, but the EU is still empty handed. Where is our climate target? Where is our contribution to the global COP agenda?
And at the same time, country after country failed to reach their climate goals, and they're all pretending that it's not a big deal. But the targets of Sweden, of Denmark, of France, Romania, Bulgaria and Germany, they sum up to the EU target and the EU target is aligned according to the Paris Agreement. So when your country fails on your national targets, you are failing on the EU target and you are betraying the Paris Agreement.
I am going to Brazil with a clear message to Ulf Kristersson and the other heads of state and government who avoid responsibility: it's time to wake up. It is time to show leadership and show that you are supporting the Paris Agreement.

'Polexit'! Let's regain our independence. This will be much easier since you keep repeating your false doctrines: Green Deal; rainbow deal; migration pact; warmongering. Thank you very much. Life is going to be much better after we leave the European Union. Down with euroko脜聜choz!

First, let me reiterate our strong support for the incoming COP presidency. The European Union will continue to engage with all it can to achieve success on all the various levers that are important in Bel脙漏m. We will be there. We will be there front and centre. We will be building coalitions around a shared commitment towards the Paris Agreement.
Climate change does not discriminate, and that means this truly should be a joint effort. And again, leadership is about articulating what it is that others can expect from us, but it is also articulating what we expect from others. Many mentioned the numbers again. Europe has done quite a lot in the past. It now stands at 6脗聽% of global emissions. And that is not to say that we do not need to do more to tackle those 6脗聽%, but we cannot fix this alone. And we do need to make sure that those responsible for the larger parts of the 94脗聽% simply do more.
Secondly, and Madam President, I would hope you would allow me to respond to a couple of things that came up frequently in the debate. And first, I'm going to say something that many brought up 芒聙聯 Ms脗聽Pereira, Ms脗聽Schilling, Mr脗聽Liese, Mr脗聽L脙鲁pez and others 芒聙聯 and that was about, you know, the process, but also the content of the ambition as the European Union is putting it on the table.
Indeed, as quite often is the case in politics, procedures and trajectories and processes are never completely straightforward. And that is what we are also currently experiencing in the debate about 2035 and 2040. And as much as I would like to see a straight line between A and B, that is just simply very often the case. Does it matter? That is also a question that others asked: does it matter internationally? My read is it doesn't, as long as we come up with ambition towards a 2035 target.
That is also my experience from a couple of weeks ago in New York. Processes are not only complicated here in Europe. What people judge is the number we bring to the table. And to all those in the international arena that have asked questions, I am saying: please do your utmost to basically match our ambition, match our range, match our targets, join us in basically tackling this.
Specifically, and we had that conversation already 芒聙聯 that will be the second question or set of questions I wanted to respond to 芒聙聯 Madam President, is the one Emma Wiesner also asked in the more confined session we had earlier today. And that is something like the global stocktake that has a rhythm of every five years, would it not be good to take stock more frequently? And I think there is merit in that logic, because once in every five years with a problem that is getting so out of hand, it actually does make sense. So I am going to look into it. But I'm also going to say that I first want to see the exact ramifications, because this is, again, something you cannot do alone. You would need to do it with the global community. So maybe there are shortcuts, maybe there are ways to have that debate without all the procedures that are necessary, but I'm at least intellectually open to it.
Third, as Catarina Martins and some others have alluded to, are we speaking to all stakeholders? Absolutely. And we are doing that before the COP, at the COP and again after the COP, with NGOs, with a range of organisations, with businesses who are also on the same page as we are, with politicians domestically and of course internationally脗聽芒聙聯 basically with everyone, from all walks of life, those we very much agree with and those we might not yet agree with. And that is what diplomacy is all about, right? Talking to those you agree with and those with whom you have things to discuss.
Finally, and there were more and more who moved a bit into things that are only loosely related to this debate, but I do want to set the record straight on one thing. Alexandr Vondra and others have asked again about nuclear, and I just want to be very straightforward here. The European Union 芒聙聯 and it is up to Member States 芒聙聯 but the European Union has embraced and continues to embrace nuclear as part of the solution space. So you can continue to ask for it, but you already have it.

I also understand those of you who regret that we have not yet reached an agreement on the 2040 target, but I assure you that that is still the aim of the Danish Presidency. The Presidency has called a possible additional Council meeting on the 4 November. The aim of this meeting will be to reach an agreement on the 2040 target and, subsequently, the NDC, in order to be ready for COP30 芒聙聯 of course, without prejudging the European Council outcome.
We are, as a Danish Presidency, very well aware that COP30 takes place in the context of deepening climate crisis. We see the devastating consequences of climate change across the globe. Drought, floods, rising sea levels and extreme weather events are already disrupting livelihoods for millions of people. Decisive climate action is more urgent than ever. It must be coupled with societal, industrial and technological transition. The EU is well on track to achieve its 2030 climate target and is leading the way in international climate negotiations. But our efforts can only be effective on a global scale if we are joined by others. We will continue our international engagement towards a meaningful outcome of COP30.

The debate is closed.
The vote will take place tomorrow, 23脗聽October 2025.
Written Statements (Rule 178)

The EU has failed to show leadership ahead of the upcoming UN Climate Change Conference due to political resistance that denies the urgent need and the economic rationality behind climate action. The European Parliament and the Council have been unable to agree on the positions for the European Climate Law targets for 2040, and the EU has failed to deliver its NDCs for 2035 on time. If the EU does not lead in climate action, who will?

The climate change is not a political decision but a scientific fact. Whether we choose to solve the crisis or not, however, is a political decision. Closing our eyes to the symptoms of accelerating climate change does not make the reality disappear. The climate is changing, and our ecosystems are degrading due to human caused activities.

COP30 will be another chance for the international community to agree on the necessary actions to stop climate change. Nevertheless, this will not be possible if the EU maintains a weak and unambitious position. We need to step up - not only for the EU and its citizens, but also for the international community that relies on the EU.

Our common objectives at the conference follow our broader conservation goals, especially those in the EU biodiversity strategy for 2030, the revised EU action plan against wildlife trafficking and the European Green Deal. The EU's key objectives at CoP20 concern two main areas: to regulate international trade in endangered animal and plant species, pursuing a science-based approach, and to strengthen the response by the international community to wildlife trafficking. When it comes to regulating international trade in endangered animal and plant species, the EU is committed to stronger measures to combat illegal wildlife trade, especially for species at high risk. This includes aquatic species like eels and marine sharks.
The EU position is in line with the principles in the Parliament's draft resolution, notably the focus on stronger law enforcement, greater international cooperation and improved abilities to trace wildlife products. In addition, we will urge all CITES parties to adopt the strongest possible measures to protect species such as the European eel. The Council and the European Parliament agree that the protection of endangered species must be based on science. At CoP20, the EU will prioritise the adoption of proposals based on robust scientific evidence, especially those about species that are facing unsustainable trade levels. We will also consider listing proposals concerning endemic species, which are particularly vulnerable due to their limited geographical distribution and often small population sizes.
When it comes to strengthening the response by the international community to wildlife trafficking, the EU recognises the importance of aligning the CITES objectives with the global biodiversity agenda, particularly the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. This includes aligning with its targets, stopping human-caused extinction and promoting sustainable wildlife management. We must also increase synergies between CITES and the Convention on Biological Diversity and the UN General Assembly resolutions on illegal trafficking. Last but not least, we recognise the critical role of enforcement authorities and we support capacity-building. We must prosecute wildlife trafficking and improve compliance.
Let me now address the second part of the question on the implementation of the revised EU action plan against wildlife trafficking. Since its adoption in 2022, it has been the cornerstone of our strategy to fight the global crisis of wildlife trafficking. The action plan focuses on four main priorities: preventing wildlife trafficking and addressing its root causes; strengthening the legal and policy framework; enforcing regulations and policies, and strengthening global partnerships. Progress has been made in several areas. Multi-agency cooperation is stronger. As a result, we have more prevention, more detection, more prosecution and more sanction of wildlife crimes. The EU has also taken steps to treat wildlife trafficking as a serious crime. This is complemented by capacity-building and specialisation across the enforcement chain.
Challenges remain, however. Despite increased enforcement, wildlife trafficking is still a serious threat to biodiversity and security. Changes in trading routes and the increased use of online platforms for illegal wildlife trade are new challenges that require new solutions. The EU is committed to addressing these challenges by stronger legal frameworks, enhanced enforcement and global partnerships.

Just last week, we secured the G20 ministers' declaration on crimes affecting the environment. AT COP脗聽20, the EU will present proposals to include additional species to better regulate their trade and to protect them from overexploitation. These proposals are grounded in science, backed by data and supported by our shared duty to act before it's too late. But each new listing increases the complexity of enforcement and the burden of authorities. We will therefore call for practical solutions to help parties strengthen compliance, build capacity and ensure that the CITES secretariat and committees can fulfil their mandates effectively.
Honourable Members, the EU's leadership in CITES is not limited to these negotiations. It extends to what we do every day across our Union and with partners worldwide to combat illegal wildlife trade. The first progress report on the implementation of the revised EU action脗聽plan against wildlife trafficking in 2023 shows that the EU and its Member States have made steady progress in translating commitment into actions. Member States have strengthened their legal frameworks, introduced stricter penalties and integrated wildlife crime into judicial training.
Awareness campaigns have reached airports, schools and local communities, reminding citizens that buying illegal wildlife products fuels global crime and harms biodiversity. Let me also thank the Members that have supported this awareness-raising across Europe. The report also highlights major enforcement successes. For example, in 2023, Operation Lake, coordinated by Europol, led to the arrest of 256脗聽people for trafficking in glass eels, and Operation Thunder mobilised authorities from 133脗聽countries to tackle forestry and wildlife crimes. These actions show that international cooperation works and must continue.
However, as Ms Ripa said, challenges remain: convictions for financial crimes linked to wildlife trafficking are rare. Cross-border investigations 芒聙聯 particularly those targeting online 芒聙聯 are too few, and data on prosecutions and penalties remain limited. To address these gaps, the Commission continues to implement the action plan, promote cross-border collaboration and test innovative solutions. For example, a pilot project entrusted by the European Parliament is exploring market-based interventions to reduce demand for illegal sourced exotic pets. Results are expected next year. In 2026, the Commission will also follow up on studies on the criminalisation of illegally sourced wildlife, and an EU-wide positive list for animals allowed to be kept as pets.
President, honourable Members, Minister, CITES脗聽COP脗聽20 comes at a pivotal moment and multilateralism must deliver. The EU will go to Samarkand ready to work constructively with all parties to uphold science, enhance enforcement and support countries that need capacity to act. Our message is simple: protecting wildlife is protecting our societies and our future. It safeguards ecosystems that sustain our food, water and climate. It preserves a living legacy for generations to come. So let us continue to stand together 芒聙聯 European Parliament, Member States and partners 芒聙聯 to ensure that the EU remains a global force for nature and wildlife.
I am confident that with your continued support, the EU will lead by example 芒聙聯 standing firm for species protection, sustainable trade and effective multilateralism.

CITES is an important treaty. It covers roughly 40脗聽000 species worldwide, yet countless others don't fall under this regulation. Therefore, we need a strong CITES and we need a strong enforcement chain to tackle illegal trade in wildlife.
We need the European Union to be strong and successful at CoP20. Therefore, Renew Europe supports the final position text. We call for the review of CITES strategic vision to align with Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework and ask to strengthen legal frameworks against wildlife trafficking. If we need to stop wildlife crime, we simply need to act. The time is now!

The use and trade of wildlife is not only a matter of animal welfare and biodiversity preservation, but also a major threat to public health, due to the risk of future zoonotic diseases. So a One Health approach must be a priority at the upcoming CoP20 meeting in Uzbekistan. This is closely linked to the urgent need for the EU-wide positive list.
Last but not least, let me say that I am deeply concerned about EU exports of wild animals, including certain species, to the Vantara zoological complex in India. And yet, I call on the Commission to suspend exports to Vantara脗聽and push for stricter CITES enforcement at the CoP20.

Today our message is the same. Our resolution holds firm. Delivering on this commitment is not only vital for Europe's energy security, it is vital for a cleaner energy system and a more competitive economy. It accompanies and accelerates our efforts to roll out more clean energy, to improve energy efficiency and to build a truly integrated and interconnected energy union.
It is not simply about closing the tab on Russia. It is about opening the flow of home-grown, affordable, clean energy across our continent. Our roadmap outlines the steps that must be taken to achieve these goals, and it is essential that we take these steps together.
Therefore, I want to sincerely thank you, the honourable Members of this House, for your commitment and your efforts on this crucial file. In particular, I want to thank the ITRE and the INTA teams for their good cooperation, and I congratulate you on your swift adoption脗聽of the Parliament's position.
As you know, the Council reached a general approach two days ago: both co-legislators deserve huge credit for advancing these files so quickly. I fully share the view that we need robust monitoring and enforcement to avoid any attempts at circumvention, and I am fully aware that this can be done without creating unnecessary administrative burdens or barriers to gas imports from non-Russian suppliers.
Building on another point raised, it is also important to ensure a gradual and coordinated phase-out of remaining fossil fuel imports from Russia. Even if Russian oil only accounts for 3脗聽% of our imports in Europe, that is still 3脗聽% too much. It is important to note that each of our Member States who continue to import Russian oil have viable alternatives. The Commission will support those Member States to embrace these alternatives and to fully phase out the reliance on Russian oil by working through their mandatory diversification plans. Additionally, work on our proposal for nuclear is ongoing, and we are engaged in close dialogue with Member States and key stakeholders.
Honourable Members, let me thank you once again for your commitment on this file. We now have strong political momentum, it is essential that we maintain this momentum so that we can deliver on our shared ambition to adopt this proposal by the end of the year. My team and I are ready to fully commit to work with you on achieving this ambition. I am confident that we will reach fair compromises, find good solutions and fulfil our shared goal for a more secure, sustainable and competitive Europe.

We are now finally ready in Europe to forever untie ourselves from this dependency. That is why this legislation to ban Russian gas imports and also the Parliament's inclusion to ban also oil imports is historic. In 2024, we were still importing fossil fuels from Russia to the value of EUR脗聽22脗聽billion 芒聙聯 income that Russia uses to feed its war economy and its atrocious war on Ukraine. This is unacceptable. We must use sanctions to stop Russia's war, but we also must go beyond sanctions.
The European Parliament has adopted a position that not only bans forever Russian gas and oil imports, but it also does it with strict timetables, strict enforcement and closing of all loopholes and without exemptions, targeting also the shadow fleet and other efforts of circumvention by Russia. This is timely and this is super important. At the same time, we need to build the future as we quit the past. We need to build a sustainable European energy system based on renewables, energy efficiency, electrification and other clean European solutions. This is also about competitiveness, it is about climate action and it is about protecting democracy against the fossil dictators.
We need to convince the Council in the trilogues to have the same ambition to quit Russian fossil fuels once and for all.
